title: Multi-Agent Systems
lesson:
A single AI Agent is one worker: a language model that can think, call tools, and loop until a job is done. A multi-agent system is a team of these workers. Each often has its own job, its own system prompt (the hidden instructions that set its role), and sometimes its own tools. They talk to each other to finish a bigger task that one agent would fumble.

Two analogies to hold the shape

Think of a restaurant kitchen. One cook can do everything in a quiet cafe: take the order, chop, grill, plate, wash up. But during a dinner rush you split the work — a head chef who reads tickets and decides what happens, a grill station, a sauce station, a dishwasher. Each person is narrow and fast; the head chef coordinates. That is a multi-agent system, with the language model playing each role.

The second picture is a company org chart. There is usually a manager (the orchestrator or supervisor — the agent that breaks the goal into sub-tasks and hands them out) and several workers (the sub-agents — each doing one focused piece). The manager does not do the typing; it delegates, collects results, and decides what is next.

Why bother? Why not one big prompt?

  • Focus beats sprawl. A model given one clear role with a short, tidy context window (the limited text it can read at once) makes fewer mistakes than one juggling ten goals.
  • Parallel speed. Three sub-agents can research three topics at the same time, then report back. One agent must do them one after another.
  • Specialisation. A "researcher", a "coder", and a "critic" can each have different tools, different models, even different temperatures (how random the output is).
  • Easier to debug. When something breaks you can inspect one agent's input and output in isolation, instead of untangling a single giant prompt that tried to do everything at once.

How they talk

Agents do not share a brain. They share messages — text passed between them, exactly like emails between coworkers. The manager sends a sub-agent a task; the sub-agent does work (maybe calling tools via Tool Calling) and sends back a result.

Three common shapes show up again and again:

PatternWhat happens
Orchestrator + workersOne boss splits the task, hands pieces to specialists, merges answers. Most common.
Pipeline (assembly line)Agent A's output feeds Agent B feeds Agent C. Good for fixed stages: draft → edit → fact-check.
Debate / criticOne agent proposes, another challenges it, they iterate to a better answer.

Notice the overlap with Agentic Workflows: a workflow is the script (fixed steps you wrote), while a multi-agent system lets agents decide who does what at runtime. Real products mix both — predictable rails where you can, free decisions where you must.

A tiny worked example

A research assistant. The manager reads a question, spawns specialist sub-agents, then a writer agent stitches the findings together.

def manager(question):
    # 1. Break the goal into focused sub-tasks
    subtasks = plan(question)          # e.g. ['costs', 'risks', 'alternatives']
    # 2. Run worker agents in parallel, each with its own role prompt
    findings = run_parallel([
        researcher(role=t, query=question) for t in subtasks
    ])
    # 3. Hand all findings to a writer agent to merge into one answer
    return writer(findings, question)

Each researcher and the writer is its own agent: a fresh model call with its own instructions and its own clean context. The manager never reads raw web pages — it only sees tidy summaries. That keeps every agent's context small and sharp, which is the whole point.

💡 Start with ONE agent. Only split into a team when you can name the exact bottleneck a second agent removes. A team adds cost, latency, and new ways to fail.

The honest downsides

More agents means more model calls, so more money and more waiting. Errors compound: if each agent is 90% reliable, five in a chain is roughly 0.9⁵ ≈ 59% reliable end-to-end. Agents can also talk past each other, repeat work, or loop forever.

This is why frameworks like LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and the OpenAI Agents SDK exist — they handle the message-passing, retries, and stop conditions so you focus on the roles. This is closely tied to AI Orchestration Systems, the plumbing that runs the whole team reliably.
mentalModel: A multi-agent system is a kitchen brigade: one head chef (the orchestrator) reads the order and delegates, while focused stations (sub-agents) each do one job fast and pass plates back. No shared brain — they coordinate by passing messages.
takeaways: A team of focused agents, coordinated by a manager — not one do-it-all agent.
They share messages, not memory: each hand-off is text.
Teams buy focus and parallel speed, but cost more and compound errors.
mistakes: Reaching for a team too early. Most tasks are solved by one good agent with the right tools. Add agents only to remove a specific, named bottleneck — not because 'more agents' sounds powerful.
Forgetting errors multiply. Five agents at 90% reliability each give roughly 59% end-to-end, not 90%. Add checks, retries, and a critic agent, or keep chains short.
No stop condition. Agents that hand work back and forth can loop forever, burning tokens and money. Always cap the number of turns and the total spend.
Dumping the whole conversation into every agent. Each agent should get only the slice of context it needs. Stuffing every message into every context window is slow, expensive, and confuses the model.
Vague roles. 'You are a helpful assistant' for all five agents means no real specialisation. Give each a sharp, distinct system prompt and a clear hand-off format.
Letting agents pass messy free text. If sub-agents return raw, unstructured blobs, the manager can't reliably merge them. Ask for structured output (e.g. JSON) so hand-offs are machine-readable.

diagram.caption: The manager splits the goal, hands pieces to specialist sub-agents in parallel, then merges their results into one answer.

quiz.q: In an orchestrator-plus-workers system, what does the orchestrator (manager) mainly do?
options: Does all the typing and tool calls itself, then hides the workers
Breaks the goal into sub-tasks, delegates them, and merges the results
Stores a shared memory that every agent reads and writes directly
Acts as a faster model that replaces the need for sub-agents
quiz.explain: The manager coordinates: it plans, hands focused tasks to workers, and combines what they return. It delegates rather than doing the detailed work itself.

quiz.q: Five agents run in a chain, each about 90% reliable on its step. Roughly how reliable is the whole chain end-to-end?
options: About 90% — the weakest step sets the limit
About 98% — agents catch each other's mistakes
About 59% — the errors multiply across steps
quiz.explain: Errors compound: 0.9 to the 5th power is roughly 0.59. This is why long agent chains need critics, retries, or fewer links.

quiz.q: How do agents in a multi-agent system actually share information?
options: They share one giant memory that all of them edit at once
They pass messages (text) back and forth, like emails between coworkers
They are merged into a single neural network at runtime
They read each other's GPU memory directly for speed
quiz.explain: Agents do not share a brain or memory. Each hand-off is plain text sent from one agent to another, which is why structured, tidy messages matter.

quiz.q: Your single agent handles a customer-support FAQ task well today. When is it worth splitting into a multi-agent team?
options: Immediately — more agents always means better results
When you can name a specific bottleneck a second agent removes
Never — multi-agent systems are only academic
Only once you have spare GPU budget, regardless of need
quiz.explain: Start with one agent. A team adds cost, latency, and new failure modes, so only add agents to fix a concrete, named bottleneck — not on principle.

exercise:
Design (on paper or in a diagram) a 3-agent system for the task: "Write a short, accurate blog post about a topic I give you." Name each agent, write a one-line role for each, and draw the arrows showing what message flows between them. Aim for a Researcher (gathers facts), a Writer (drafts the post), and a Fact-Checker / Critic (flags anything unsupported and sends it back for a fix). Decide your stop condition: how many revision rounds before you ship?

Stretch goal: Sketch the same task as a single-agent agentic workflow instead. Write two sentences on which version you'd actually build first, and why — be honest about cost and reliability.
solution:
One workable design: Manager receives the topic and tells the Researcher to return 5–8 bullet facts with sources. Those bullets go to the Writer, which drafts ~300 words using only those facts. The draft plus the bullets go to the Fact-Checker, which returns either approved or a list of unsupported claims. If not approved, the manager sends the issues back to the Writer (cap at, say, 2 revision rounds, then ship the best version). Each agent gets only what it needs — the Writer never sees raw web pages, only clean bullets.

On the stretch goal: For a first build, the single-agent workflow is usually the right call — it's cheaper, faster to debug, and 'good enough' for many blog posts. Reach for the 3-agent team once you measure a real quality gap (e.g. the lone agent keeps inventing facts) that a dedicated Fact-Checker would catch.
summary: A multi-agent system is a team of AI agents — usually a manager that delegates and specialist sub-agents that each do one focused job — coordinating by passing messages, not by sharing a brain. Teams buy you focus, parallel speed, and specialisation, but cost money, add latency, and let errors compound. Start with one agent; split into a team only to remove a bottleneck you can name, and always set hard stop conditions.

keyTerms.def: A group of AI agents that work together on a task, each often with its own role, instructions, and tools, coordinating by passing messages.
The agent that breaks the goal into sub-tasks, hands them to worker agents, and merges the results. Acts like a team lead.
An agent that handles one focused piece of the larger task and reports back to the orchestrator.
How agents communicate — by sending text back and forth, like emails between coworkers. They don't share memory.
The moment one agent passes its result (ideally in a tidy, structured format) to the next agent or back to the manager.
An assembly-line arrangement where one agent's output feeds straight into the next, through fixed stages (e.g. draft → edit → fact-check).
An arrangement where one agent proposes an answer and another challenges it, iterating to improve quality.
When mistakes multiply across a chain of agents, so several fairly reliable agents in a row can produce an unreliable end result.
A hard limit — max turns, max revisions, or max spend — that prevents agents from looping forever.
